us-politics.headline: Trump Escalates Iran War as Budget Prioritises Defence
us-politics.body: The US military has struck Iranian targets, with Trump warning attacks on bridges and power plants are coming. Iran retaliated by hitting Gulf refineries, tightening its grip on the Strait of Hormuz - a chokepoint that gives Tehran real leverage over global oil flows. Markets were unimpressed by Trump's prime-time push for the war. The White House simultaneously released a budget proposal seeking up to $1.5 trillion for defence, offset by deep cuts to domestic programmes. Supporters argue the spending reflects genuine strategic need; critics say the trade-off punishes ordinary Americans. B-52 deployments signal the conflict is expanding in scope.

sources.title: Trump calls for a major increase in defense spending alongside cuts in domestic spending
Live updates: White House budget expected, with up to $1.5T for defense
Trump: US military ‘hasn’t even started destroying what’s left in Iran’
Iran hits Gulf refineries as Trump warns U.S. will attack Iranian bridges, power plants
Iran ‘toll booth’ on Strait of Hormuz underscores its leverage on Trump, US
Trump pushed the Iran war in prime time. Investors weren’t swayed.
What B-52 bombers bring to Iran fight — and what it means for the war now

us-politics.headline: Bondi Out at DOJ, Epstein Inquiry Future Uncertain
us-politics.body: Attorney General Pam Bondi has been removed, with analysts pointing to her failure to deliver high-profile prosecutions Trump wanted. Trump's aide Todd Blanche denied the Epstein files played any role in her departure. Democrats are sceptical. Representative Ro Khanna warned the shake-up could bury the Epstein inquiry just as it gained momentum, and NPR reports that despite file releases, arrests remain scarce. Five major questions now hang over the DOJ: who leads it, what happens to ongoing cases, and whether the Epstein investigation survives the transition. The White House has not named a permanent replacement.

sources.title: Blanche: I never heard Trump say Bondi exit ‘had anything to do with the Epstein files’
Whole Hog Politics: Bondi beached
Rep. Ro Khanna, D-Calif., on the future of the Epstein inquiry with Bondi's ousting
Five questions hanging over the DOJ shake-up
The Memo: Bondi’s failure to get Trump his ‘scalps’ sealed her fate
After the release of the Epstein files, why have there been so few arrests?

us-politics.headline: Jobs Beat Forecasts, but Political Headwinds Persist
us-politics.body: The US added 178,000 jobs in March, well above expectations - a number both parties will fight over heading into midterms. Republicans will claim economic momentum; Democrats point to inflation and war costs eating into household budgets. A new poll shows mixed signals for Democrats hoping to retake Congress, with redistricting battles in Virginia adding further uncertainty. Meanwhile, Palantir-linked donations are putting some Democrats in an awkward spot on immigration technology, and a lawsuit alleging racial discrimination in Congressional Black Caucus scholarships adds another fault line inside the party.

sources.title: US adds 178K jobs in March, far more than expected
Palantir-linked campaign donations put Democrats in tight spot
Poll shows mixed signs for Democratic push to gain seats in Congress
Black Caucus scholarships discriminate based on race, lawsuit alleges

us-politics.headline: Gabbard Rumours, Vance in Hungary, and GOP 2028 Jostling
us-politics.body: The White House dismissed reports that Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard is about to be removed, calling them fake news. The denial did little to quiet speculation. Vice President Vance travels to Hungary to meet Viktor Orbán days before parliamentary elections there, a visit that will draw scrutiny given Orbán's alignment with Moscow amid the Iran conflict. Analysts are already mapping a potential 2028 primary clash between Vance and Secretary of State Rubio, with the chaos of the Trump era shaping both men's positioning. The Republican field is forming earlier than usual.

sources.title: White House calls reports of Gabbard’s impending ouster ‘fake news’
The Vance versus Rubio 2028 nomination fight through the lens of chaos
VP Vance to meet with Viktor Orbán in Hungary days ahead of foreign nation's elections

au-politics.headline: Fuel Crisis Bites as Industry Warns Stocks Won't Last
au-politics.body:
Australia's fuel security is under serious pressure, with industry warning that current stocks could run out within a month. The crisis is tied to broader disruption across Asia, where the Iran war has squeezed supply chains and left ordinary workers scrambling for fuel. The Albanese government has faced sharp questions over its preparedness.

Energy Minister Chris Bowen has moved to update the public, but industry groups say the response falls short. The situation puts a spotlight on Australia's long-standing vulnerability - the country imports the vast majority of its refined fuel and holds among the lowest strategic reserves of any developed nation. With no quick fix in sight, the pressure on Canberra is mounting fast.

sources.title: Fuel heading to Australia won't last a month, industry says
A day in the life of Asia’s fuel crisis

au-politics.headline: Trump Fires Senior Officials in Sweeping Administration Purge
au-politics.body:
Donald Trump has moved aggressively to reshape his administration, dismissing a string of senior officials including Attorney-General Pam Bondi. The firings extend a pattern of loyalty-testing that has accelerated in recent months, with some of Trump's most prominent female defenders now among those removed.

Analysts are divided on what the purge signals. Supporters argue Trump is tightening control to deliver on his agenda. Critics say the instability undermines governance and reflects an administration increasingly intolerant of internal dissent. Separately, Trump's 100% tariff on pharmaceuticals imported into the US has drawn a firm response from Canberra, with Australia ruling out passing any cost increases on to consumers or the PBS.

sources.title: Donald Trump is going on a firing spree
Trump's fiercest female defenders are now on the outer
Australia says it won’t raise drug prices after Trump’s 100% tariff on pharmaceuticals imported into US

au-politics.headline: Westpac Boss Flags Recession Risk as Economic Pressure Grows
au-politics.body:
Westpac chief executive Andrew Miller has warned there is a genuine chance of recession in Australia, citing global uncertainty and the slow pace of interest rate relief. The comments carry weight - Westpac is one of the country's four major banks, and public warnings of this kind from bank chiefs are rare.

The remarks land as households remain under sustained cost-of-living pressure and the Reserve Bank moves cautiously on rates. Global factors, including Trump's tariff push and the fuel supply crunch, are adding to the headwinds. The timing, ahead of the federal election, makes the economic outlook a live political battleground for both major parties.

sources.title: 'There's a chance of a recession,' warns Westpac boss

au-politics.headline: Easter Road Toll Rises as Fatal Crashes Hit WA and NSW
au-politics.body:
At least four people have died in separate crashes across Western Australia and New South Wales over the Easter long weekend. A 14-year-old dirt bike rider was among three killed in WA, while a motorcyclist died in a crash on the Monaro Highway in southern NSW, forcing the road's closure.

Police in both states have urged drivers to slow down and rest regularly. The deaths come despite longstanding public safety campaigns targeting the Easter period, which consistently records some of the year's highest road fatality numbers. Authorities say fatigue and speed remain the dominant factors.

sources.title: Highway closed after motorcyclist dies in crash in southern NSW
Dirt bike rider, 14, among three dead in separate WA Easter long weekend crashes

world.headline: US-Israel War on Iran Draws War Crimes Allegations
world.body:
Over 100 American legal experts have condemned US strikes on Iran as possible war crimes, citing attacks on civilian infrastructure including a bridge in Karaj. International law scholars are raising alarms as the conflict stretches on with no clear end in sight, while Iran accuses Washington and Tel Aviv of a deliberate campaign targeting hospitals, medicine supplies, and vaccines.

Iranians on the ground describe mounting fear after each new strike. Kuwait reported missile and drone hits on a desalination plant and oil refinery, signalling the conflict is spilling beyond Iran's borders. Iran condemned what it called a US-Israeli moral collapse, as debate intensifies over whether the Trump administration has a coherent exit strategy.

sources.title: How the US and Israel are waging war on Iran’s medicines, vaccines
War crimes are no longer shameful. That should terrify you
International law experts allege violations in Iran war
When will the Iran war end? Tracing the Trump administration's timelines
Over 100 US legal experts condemn strikes on Iran as possible ‘war crimes’
'This has got me worried': Iranians fear what comes next after US strike on Karaj bridge
Kuwait desalination plant, oil refinery hit by missile and drone strikes
Iran condemns US-Israeli ‘moral collapse’ after attacks on civilian sites

world.headline: Trump Purges Army Chief and Attorney General in One Day
world.body:
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth forced out the US Army's top general, with General Randy George replaced by Christopher LaNeve in what appears to be a broader sweep of senior military leadership. The dismissal follows a pattern of Hegseth removing commanders seen as insufficiently loyal to the administration's agenda.

Separately, Trump fired Attorney General Pam Bondi and moved to install Todd Blanche, his former personal defense lawyer, as her replacement. The back-to-back removals of a senior general and the nation's top law enforcement officer in a single day mark an aggressive consolidation of executive control.

sources.title: Pete Hegseth asks US Army's top general to step down
Who is Christopher LaNeve, set to lead the US Army?
Trump removes US Attorney General Pam Bondi
Why did Trump fire Pam Bondi from Justice Department, who is Todd Blanche?
Hegseth fires US Army chief of staff in reported string of dismissals

world.headline: Artemis II Breaks Earth Orbit Heading for the Moon
world.body: NASA's Artemis II mission fired its engines and left Earth's orbit on schedule, putting four astronauts on course for the far side of the Moon. It is the first crewed lunar mission since Apollo 17 in 1972. The spacecraft is expected to loop around the Moon before returning to Earth, serving as a critical test ahead of a planned lunar landing.

sources.title: Artemis II leaves Earth's orbit on track for far side of the Moon
Artemis II mission fires up engines in next step of moon mission

world.headline: Burkina Faso Junta Tells Citizens to Abandon Democracy
world.body:
Burkina Faso's military leader Ibrahim Traore told his country to forget democracy, doubling down on the junta's rejection of civilian governance since seizing power in 2022. Traore framed democratic systems as foreign impositions incompatible with Burkina Faso's current security crisis, where jihadist violence has displaced millions.

The statement marks one of the most explicit rejections of democratic norms by any sitting head of state in recent memory, and deepens concern over West Africa's democratic backsliding as multiple military governments in the Sahel consolidate power.

sources.title: Burkina Faso military leader Traore says ‘forget democracy’
Burkina Faso must 'forget' about democracy, military leader says

ai.headline: OpenAI Buys a Talk Show to Control Its Own Narrative
ai.body:
OpenAI has acquired TBPN, a founder-focused business talk show with a loyal tech-industry audience. The deal is nakedly strategic: rather than earning favorable coverage, OpenAI is simply buying the outlet that produces it. Critics are already framing this as a media capture play dressed up as a content investment.

The acquisition raises real questions about editorial independence and what it means when an AI company owns the microphone. TBPN's hosts built credibility by being plugged into startup culture. That credibility now belongs to OpenAI. Expect the show to continue looking independent while functioning as something closer to in-house PR.

sources.title: OpenAI Acquires Tech Talk Show ‘TBPN’—and Buys Itself Some Positive News
OpenAI acquires TBPN, the buzzy founder-led business talk show
OpenAI just bought TBPN

ai.headline: AI Chatbots Are Now Prescribing Psychiatric Medication
ai.body:
AI chatbots are moving into clinical territory, with some platforms now prescribing and refilling psychiatric drugs for patients. This is not a hypothetical risk. It is happening. The implications for patient safety are serious, particularly for medications that require careful monitoring and dosage adjustment.

Separately, a lawsuit against Perplexity alleges its "incognito mode" offers no real privacy protection, exposing a pattern of AI companies overstating user safeguards. And a privacy warning around Granola, an AI note-taking app, revealed that meeting notes are publicly accessible by default to anyone with a link. Across these stories, the throughline is the same: AI products are moving fast and burying the risks in the fine print.

sources.title: Chatbots are now prescribing psychiatric drugs
PSA: Anyone with a link can view your Granola notes by default
Perplexity's "Incognito Mode" is a "sham," lawsuit says

ai.headline: New Rowhammer Attack Hands Hackers Full GPU Control
ai.body:
Researchers have demonstrated a new class of Rowhammer attacks that can give attackers complete control of machines running Nvidia GPUs. Rowhammer exploits work by repeatedly accessing memory rows to flip bits in adjacent rows, bypassing software-level defenses entirely. GPU-accelerated infrastructure - the backbone of modern AI workloads - is now squarely in the crosshairs.

The timing is uncomfortable. As data centers race to pack in more GPU capacity, this attack surface grows with them. Google's announcement of a new data center powered by a large gas plant underlines just how much physical and digital infrastructure is being built out right now, and how much is at stake if the security foundations are shaky.

sources.title: New Rowhammer attacks give complete control of machines running Nvidia GPUs
A New Google-Funded Data Center Will Be Powered by a Massive Gas Plant

ai.headline: EU Folds on Digital Rules as US Pressure Mounts
ai.body:
Critics are accusing the European Union of surrendering its digital regulatory agenda under pressure from the United States. The concern is that landmark frameworks like the Digital Markets Act, designed to rein in Big Tech, are being quietly softened to avoid trade friction with Washington. For regulators who spent years building these rules, it looks like a capitulation.

Meanwhile, NHS staff in the UK are refusing to use the Federated Data Platform over ethical objections to Palantir, its primary contractor. Both stories point to the same tension: governments are struggling to hold the line on tech governance when commercial and political pressures push the other way.

sources.title: Critics say EU risks ceding control of its tech laws under U.S. pressure
NHS staff refusing to use FDP over Palantir ethical concerns

btc.headline: Bitcoin Drifts Into Holiday Weekend With Bears Circling
btc.body:
Bitcoin traded flat heading into the Good Friday long weekend, with ETF inflows and CME futures activity set to go dark for days. Futures markets tilted bearish, volatility cooled, and on-chain data showed Bitcoin supply in profit approaching levels analysts associate with true bear markets. Institutional flows that have propped up price action simply won't be there.

Cathie Wood offered a contrarian read, arguing Bitcoin is done with its historic 85% drawdown cycles and setting a $34,000 floor target. That optimism sits awkwardly against the data. Riot Platforms sold 3,778 BTC in Q1, signaling that even major miners are trimming exposure under pressure.

sources.title: CoinDesk 20 performance update: Bitcoin (BTC) trades flat while altcoins rise
Crypto snoozes into Good Friday as oil and macro stir: Crypto Daybook Americas
Crypto consolidates as volatility cools and futures markets tilt bearish
Bitcoin supply in profit heads to ‘true bear market’ levels
Bitcoin heads into holiday weekend exposed as ETF and CME flows go offline
Bitcoin 'done' with 85% crashes, says Cathie Wood amid new $34K target
Bitcoin miner Riot sold 3,778 BTC during Q1 amid broader market pressure

btc.headline: Ethereum Foundation Hits Staking Target as Quantum Threat Looms
btc.body:
The Ethereum Foundation completed a $93 million ETH staking push, reaching its self-imposed 70,000 ETH target. The move signals a shift toward the Foundation actively participating in network security rather than sitting on idle holdings.

The timing is notable. Naoris Protocol launched a quantum-resistant blockchain this week, directly citing vulnerabilities in Bitcoin and Ethereum to so-called Q-Day attacks, where sufficiently advanced quantum computers could break current cryptographic standards. Neither network has a deployed fix. The launch puts pressure on both ecosystems to accelerate post-quantum roadmaps before the threat moves from theoretical to practical.

sources.title: Ethereum Foundation stakes another $93 million ether, reaching its 70,000 ETH target
Naoris Protocol's quantum-resistant blockchain goes live as Bitcoin and Ethereum face 'Q-Day' threats

btc.headline: DeFi Loses $169M to Hacks as Drift Chases $280M Exploiter
btc.body:
DeFi protocols shed $169 million across 34 exploits in Q1 2026, according to DefiLlama, though the figure marks a decline from prior quarters. The drop reflects better auditing practices and improved protocol design, but the losses remain substantial.

Drift Protocol took an unusual step this week, sending an on-chain message directly to wallets linked to a $280 million exploit in an attempt to open negotiations. A Bank of Canada study added context to DeFi's structural risks, finding that Aave V3 avoided bad debt during volatile periods by effectively transferring liquidation losses onto borrowers rather than the protocol itself. Efficiency and risk were not eliminated - they were redistributed.

sources.title: Aave avoided bad debt by shifting risk to borrowers: Bank of Canada study
Drift sends onchain message to wallets tied to $280M exploit
Crypto hackers steal $169M from 34 DeFi protocols in Q1: DefiLlama

btc.headline: Stablecoins Overtake ACH Volume as Institutional Interest Builds
btc.body:
Stablecoins surpassed U.S. automated clearing house transaction volume in February, a milestone that puts the technology's real-world payment scale into sharp relief. Circle moved to press that advantage, announcing cirBTC, a wrapped Bitcoin token targeting institutional markets and taking direct aim at BitGo and Coinbase's competing products.

The IMF weighed in with a cautious take, acknowledging that tokenization improves financial efficiency but warning it introduces new systemic risks. Meanwhile, South Korean brokerage Korea Investment and Securities is reportedly eyeing a stake in crypto exchange Coinone, adding to a pattern of traditional financial firms seeking direct crypto market exposure.

sources.title: Stablecoins flip automated clearing house volume in February
Circle to launch cirBTC wrapped Bitcoin, challenging BitGo and Coinbase
Tokenization makes finance more efficient but introduces risks: IMF
South Korean brokerage Korea Investment & Securities eyes Coinone stake: Report

food.name: Miso-Glazed Black Cod
food.description: Silky, caramelised fish fillets marinated in white miso, mirin, and sake then broiled until lacquered and golden
food.tip: Marinate for at least 24 hours — the longer the miso sits on the fish, the deeper the umami crust when it hits the heat

exercise.name: Kettlebell Density Circuit
exercise.description: Set a timer and cycle through swings, goblet squats, and single-arm cleans every 90 seconds, tracking total rounds completed to beat next time

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — and philosophers from Hobbes to Parfit have weaponised it to interrogate whether you are the same person you were ten years ago
learn.why: On a Friday it's a surprisingly grounding rabbit hole: Derek Parfit's 'Reasons and Persons' takes the puzzle into neuroscience and mortality, and an hour with it will quietly rewire how you think about continuity, memory, and what you actually owe your past self
